risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition." Comment: This explicitly describes a truly random sequence generation method. The additional mention of stratification does not weaken this judgement because the random sequence was generated by computer-generated random numbers.

Quote: "Computer-generated random numbers were used by the site statistician at the UCSF General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) to assign group condition. After all participants had completed baseline assessments, this information was given to study staff who informed participants of their group condition." Comment: This indicates that allocation was generated and held by a statistician/site center and only communicated after baseline assessments were completed, which is an explicit mechanism preventing recruiters from foreseeing the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were not blinded to study hypotheses." Comment: The intervention was a waitlist-controlled mindfulness program with no indication that participants or personnel delivering the intervention were blinded; participants knew whether they were receiving the active mindfulness classes versus waitlist, and awareness of assignment could plausibly influence subjective outcomes (e.g., anxiety, mindfulness, eating behavior) and even behavioral measures relevant to weight/adiposity.

Quote: "Study nurses, blind to participant condition, performed the anthropometric and body composition assessments and blood draws. Research assistants administered the computerized questionnaires and provided instructions for the home saliva sampling procedure, but were not blind to participant condition at posttreatment assessments." Comment: The main objectively measured outcomes relevant here (anthropometric/body composition measures, blood draws, cortisol assays, DEXA) were assessed by nurses who were explicitly blind to allocation, and the laboratory-based measures are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of group assignment. Although research assistants were not blinded for questionnaire administration, the key objective outcomes were blinded and not dependent on assessor judgement, so detection bias for these outcomes is low.

Quote: "Five treatment and two control participants were lost to followup for the primary analysis" and "missing data at postintervention were imputed using preintervention values." Comment: Attrition was modest overall (7/47 randomized, about 15%) and only slightly imbalanced between groups (5 vs 2). The paper also states an intention-to-treat analysis with imputation of missing postintervention values using baseline values, and there is no signal that dropouts were related to efficacy or adverse events. This is reasonably reassuring for attrition bias.
